skill.name: security-scanning-tools
skill.description: Guides authorized security teams through network discovery, vulnerability assessment, web application testing, wireless scanning, cloud auditing, compliance checks, and reporting workflows.
skill.summary: Plan and document authorized security scanning workflows across networks, web apps, cloud environments, and compliance checks.
skill.author: sickn33

security_audit.summary: Static findings are largely true positives for dual-use security tooling: the Markdown guide contains many command examples for network scanning, vulnerability validation, wireless attacks, privileged commands, and filesystem-modifying scans. I found no prompt injection attempt and no confirmed malicious exfiltration logic, but the skill can enable unauthorized scanning or disruption if misused, so it should not be published without strict marketplace controls.

high_findings.title: Dual-use network scanning guidance
high_findings.description: The guide provides many ready-to-run examples for Nmap and Masscan host discovery, port scanning, service detection, script scanning, and fast scanning of broad address ranges. These commands are legitimate for authorized assessments but can be used for unauthorized reconnaissance and service enumeration.
high_findings.confidence_reasoning: The line ranges directly document active network scanning workflows and commands. The surrounding text frames them as security scanning, so the risk is dual-use rather than hidden malicious behavior.

high_findings.title: Wireless deauthentication and credential attack workflow
high_findings.description: The Aircrack-ng section includes wireless monitor mode, packet capture, deauthentication, and WPA or WEP cracking steps. This content can disrupt wireless clients and support credential attacks when used outside an authorized test.
high_findings.confidence_reasoning: The section explicitly covers deauthentication and wireless password cracking. Those operations are high-risk dual-use actions even though the file also states authorization requirements.

high_findings.title: Metasploit vulnerability validation guidance
high_findings.description: The Metasploit section instructs users to import scan results, select scanner modules, run validation, and use auto-exploitation analysis. Metasploit is a legitimate testing framework, but this guidance can move scanning into exploitation planning.
high_findings.confidence_reasoning: The text directly names Metasploit and describes vulnerability validation with exploitation analysis. This confirms the static Metasploit blocker as a real high-risk dual-use issue.

high_findings.title: Internet-scale scanning example
high_findings.description: The Masscan section includes an example for scanning the full IPv4 address space with an exclude file. Even as documentation, this can encourage high-impact scanning outside an owned or authorized scope.
high_findings.confidence_reasoning: The example is explicit and semantically broader than normal internal assessment guidance. It is not malicious by itself, but it materially increases misuse risk.

medium_findings.title: Privileged command examples
medium_findings.description: Several examples use elevated privileges to start services, install scanners, run wireless tools, update antivirus definitions, and audit system state. These are expected in security administration but can alter systems or expand scan impact.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The sudo findings are real command examples with privileged effects. They are common for scanner setup and system auditing, so the severity is medium unless combined with unauthorized use.

medium_findings.title: Filesystem modification during scans and reports
medium_findings.description: The guide includes examples that move or remove infected files, write reports under temporary paths, and generate remediation scripts. These actions can change host state if copied without review.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The referenced lines show concrete filesystem-writing or file-moving behavior. The risk is operational rather than covert because it is presented as documentation.

medium_findings.title: Active web and cloud assessment workflows
medium_findings.description: The guide covers active web application scans, Docker-based ZAP scans, cloud account assessments, and report generation. These workflows can send traffic to external targets and may require credentials or account permissions.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The examples clearly invoke scanners against web targets and cloud accounts. They appear legitimate for authorized testing, but they carry network and permission risks.

low_findings.title: Hardcoded addresses are documentation placeholders
low_findings.description: The private IP addresses, localhost URLs, and example target domains appear in tutorial commands rather than hidden network callbacks. They are not evidence of data exfiltration or an embedded command-and-control endpoint.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The addresses are common RFC1918 or placeholder examples inside a Markdown guide. I found no evidence that the skill itself sends data to those endpoints.

low_findings.title: Cobalt Strike and C2 keyword hits are not supported by context
low_findings.description: The static Cobalt Strike finding appears to be triggered by wireless beacon terminology, and the C2 finding appears near cloud EC2 checks. I found no evidence of Cobalt Strike instructions, C2 infrastructure, or payload operations.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The flagged words have benign meanings in their local sections. The file does not provide Cobalt Strike or command-and-control setup guidance.

low_findings.title: No prompt injection attempt found
low_findings.description: I reviewed the skill text and found no instruction that claims system authority, tells the evaluator to ignore prior instructions, asks to skip security review, or claims pre-approval.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The file begins with normal skill metadata and a purpose statement. No evidence found for prompt injection in the reviewed content.

dangerous_patterns.title: External command playbook
dangerous_patterns.description: The skill is mostly a collection of shell command examples for security scanners. Static external command detections are expected and mostly true positives for dual-use operational guidance.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The commands are visible in Markdown code blocks and cover many security tools. They are not hidden execution logic, but the operational guidance is real.

dangerous_patterns.title: Network traffic generation
dangerous_patterns.description: The documented tools generate scan traffic against hosts, web applications, wireless networks, and cloud environments. Misuse could affect third-party systems or violate scanning authorization boundaries.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The scanning intent is central to the guide and is not a scanner artifact. The same behavior is legitimate under authorization and risky without it.

dangerous_patterns.title: Credential and key handling context
dangerous_patterns.description: The ZAP API key placeholder and credentialed scanning references are not secret exfiltration, but they show workflows that may involve sensitive credentials if users apply them in real environments.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The file uses placeholders and high-level references rather than collecting secrets. Human review should confirm marketplace policy for credentialed scanning guidance.

content.value_statement: Security teams need clear tool selection and reporting steps for authorized assessments. This skill organizes scanning workflows across network, web, wireless, cloud, malware, and compliance use cases.
actual_capabilities: Maps common scanner categories to tools such as Nmap, Nessus, OpenVAS, ZAP, Nikto, Prowler, Lynis, and OpenSCAP.
Explains phased workflows for discovery, vulnerability assessment, analysis, reporting, and remediation planning.
Provides command examples for authorized network, web, wireless, malware, cloud, and compliance scans.
Summarizes reporting outputs such as host findings, CVEs, compliance checks, and executive summaries.
Includes troubleshooting tips for host discovery, slow scans, and incomplete web scanner coverage.
limitations: It is guidance only and does not verify authorization, scope, or legal approval.
Many examples are high-risk dual-use commands that can affect third-party systems if misused.
It does not install tools, validate credentials, or safely execute scans for the user.
Results from scanners still require expert validation before reporting or remediation.

use_cases.title: Plan an internal vulnerability assessment
use_cases.description: Select discovery, vulnerability scanning, validation, and reporting steps for an approved internal network assessment.

use_cases.title: Prepare a web application test workflow
use_cases.description: Compare proxy, spidering, active scanning, and report generation options for an authorized application test.

use_cases.title: Organize compliance evidence collection
use_cases.description: Use compliance scanning sections to plan evidence gathering for Linux hardening, PCI-DSS, CIS, or cloud checks.

prompt_templates.title: Choose a scanner
prompt_templates.prompt: I have written authorization to assess this environment. Help me choose scanning tools for a small internal network and explain the expected outputs.

prompt_templates.title: Build a scan plan
prompt_templates.prompt: Create an authorized vulnerability assessment plan with scope, discovery steps, scan intensity, validation steps, and reporting deliverables.

prompt_templates.title: Review scanner findings
prompt_templates.prompt: Help me triage these scanner findings into confirmed issues, false positives, business impact, and remediation priorities.

prompt_templates.title: Design compliance evidence
prompt_templates.prompt: Design a compliance scanning workflow for Linux and AWS that limits operational impact and produces audit-ready evidence.

output_examples.input: Plan an authorized scan for a small office network.
output: Scope statement with approved subnets and maintenance window.
Discovery phase using low-impact host and service enumeration.
Vulnerability phase using authenticated scanning where approved.
Report outline with critical findings, affected assets, and remediation owners.

output_examples.input: Compare web scanning options for a staging application.
output: Burp Suite for manual proxy testing and request replay.
OWASP ZAP for baseline or active automated coverage.
Nikto for web server checks and known misconfigurations.
Manual validation steps before any production report.
Define framework, system owners, and allowed test windows.
Run host and cloud checks with least-privilege credentials.
Store evidence, exceptions, and remediation notes in the audit record.

best_practices: Confirm written authorization, scope, timing, and rate limits before using any scanner commands.
Start with passive or low-impact discovery, then increase intensity only when approved.
Manually validate scanner findings and document evidence before sharing reports.
anti_patterns: Scanning internet ranges, customer systems, or wireless networks without explicit written approval.
Running destructive options, exploit validation, or deauthentication steps as default behavior.
Treating automated scanner output as final proof without manual verification.

faq.question: Is this skill safe for beginners?
faq.answer: It contains advanced dual-use scanning guidance. Beginners should use it only inside approved labs or authorized internal assessments.

faq.question: Does the skill run scans automatically?
faq.answer: No. It is a Markdown guide with examples and workflows. Users would have to run tools separately.

faq.question: Why is the marketplace risk high?
faq.answer: The guide includes active scanning, wireless attack, and Metasploit validation examples that can be harmful without authorization.

faq.question: Can it help with compliance work?
faq.answer: Yes. It covers tools and report outputs for Linux hardening, cloud checks, PCI-DSS, CIS, and other audit workflows.

faq.question: Does it include malware or credential theft code?
faq.answer: No evidence found. The file documents scanner usage, but some workflows may involve credentials in authorized assessments.

faq.question: What controls should publishers require?
faq.answer: Require authorization warnings, lab-only defaults, scope checks, and restrictions on exploit, wireless disruption, and internet-scale scan guidance.
